title: Beyond endoscopy and geometric terms
speaker: James Arthur
abstract: Beyond Endoscopy is the proposal by Langlands for using the trace formula to attack the Principle of Functoriality. Many of the problems at this (early) stage concern the geometric terms in the trace formula. Most immediate perhaps is to extend Altug's application of Poisson summation from $\mathrm{GL}(2)$ to more general groups. One could then try to identify the contribution of the nontempered representations on the spectral side to the resulting Fourier transforms of geometric terms.

proof_stages.label: Primary trace-formula terms for GL(n+1)
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes G=GL(n+1) and a compactly supported test function modulo the split center, then isolates the regular elliptic geometric term and the discrete spectral trace term, including the relevant inclusions among cuspidal, square-integrable, and trace-formula discrete representations.

proof_stages.label: Beyond-endoscopic filtering problem
proof_stages.summary: Langlands's proposal is formulated as detecting, on the geometric side, the cuspidal representations whose automorphic L-function L(s,π,r) has a pole at s=1, but this first requires explicitly identifying and subtracting the noncuspidal part of the discrete trace-formula spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-polynomial lattice coordinates
proof_stages.summary: After choosing test functions unramified away from infinity except for one p-adic determinant shell, regular elliptic conjugacy classes are parametrized by irreducible monic characteristic polynomials with coefficients a=(b,±p^k), rewriting the geometric term as a near-lattice sum over b.

proof_stages.label: Discriminants, volumes, and local orbital integrals
proof_stages.summary: The analysis separates Weil, polynomial, and field discriminants, uses class-number and p-adic orbital-integral formulas in the GL(2) case, and emphasizes that a higher-rank version must express local orbital integrals in a very specific arithmetic form.

proof_stages.label: Poisson summation for the GL(2) geometric side
proof_stages.summary: The Frankel-Langlands-Ngô question asks whether the regular elliptic sum can be Poisson-summed; Altug's GL(2) result answers yes after corrections, yielding a Fourier-dual expansion with analytic weights, approximate-functional-equation input, and Kloosterman-like arithmetic coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Identification of noncuspidal Fourier contribution
proof_stages.summary: Using the Poisson-transformed formula, Altug identifies the contribution of the noncuspidal discrete trace-formula representations: the zero-frequency term is exhausted by the trivial representation together with a residual induced term, up to an acceptable error.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural higher-rank dictionary
proof_stages.summary: The speaker proposes a GL(n) and more general split simply connected analogue in which exceptional spectral parameters ψ:L_F×SL_2(C)→Ĝ should correspond to Poisson-dual geometric variables through unipotent-class classifications and through an abelian class-field/Kummer-theoretic parallel.

examples.name: Classical theory of equations
examples.note: The speaker points to 19th-century invariants, resolvents, and discriminants as a familiar background for viewing elliptic conjugacy classes through characteristic polynomials, without using that theory in the argument.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes the classical theory of equations as a possible historical analogy/source of related ideas such as invariants, resolvents, and discriminants, but does not work through any such equation-theoretic example.

passes.reason: invoked as a familiar systematic background for discriminants, invariants, and resolvents, not as a special object or failure case
The classical theory of equations is invoked as a historical source of related ideas, not as a structurally special object or a refutation.

examples.name: Known GL(3) p-adic orbital-integral formulas
examples.note: Kottwitz's and Repka's GL(3) formulas are cited as instances of existing orbital-integral computations that do not yet have the divisor/local-L-factor form needed for the proposed higher-rank analysis.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites known GL(3) p-adic orbital-integral formulas by Kottwitz and Repka and comments that they are not in the desired form, without applying or deriving any of the formulas.

passes.reason: the Kottwitz/Repka GL(3) orbital-integral formulas are cited as known formulas that do not have the desired form

The known GL(3) p-adic orbital-integral formulas are cited as failing to have the desired Langlands-type form, so they function as inadequate/adversarial examples rather than structurally exceptional ones.
The existing GL(3) p-adic orbital-integral formulas are cited as failing to have the required form, illustrating an obstruction despite their availability.
Ordinary low-rank orbital-integral formulas are cited negatively because their known forms fail to have the required form for the proposed analysis.
The known GL(3) p-adic orbital-integral formulas are ordinary low-rank formulas invoked to show that existing formulas are not in the needed form.

examples.name: Jordan blocks and dual blocks for GL_n
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains the contrast between Bala-Carter and Dynkin classifications by saying that for GL_n one can use Jordan block partitions and their dual partitions to see the two parametrizations concretely.
examples.engagement_reason: He says that for GL_n Jordan normal form gives blocks and that dual blocks parameterize the related classification, but gives no concrete partition, computation, or construction on a specific GL_n case.

passes.reason: Jordan blocks and their dual partitions give the standard GL_n parametrization of unipotent classes, used illustratively rather than adversarially
Jordan blocks and their dual blocks give the standard partition-based parametrization for GL_n, with no degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or exceptional role here.
Jordan blocks and their dual blocks for GL_n are used as ordinary classification data for unipotent classes, not as a counterexample or special sporadic case.
Jordan blocks and their dual partitions give the standard systematic GL_n parametrization of unipotent classes, used illustratively rather than adversarially.

Jordan blocks and their dual partitions for GL_n are used as standard classification data for unipotent classes, not adversarially, and are not sporadic, limiting, maximal-symmetry, or optimal cases.
Used as the standard systematic parametrization of GL_n unipotent classes and its dual version, not as a refutation or sporadic/extreme case.
